1: Psalm 22 is the most cited OT Psalm in the NT Passion narratives. Its historical background (David's persecution?) is unknown — the Psalm presents an archetypal experience of abandonment and vindication. The pattern: intense lament (vv. 1-21) → sudden transition to praise (v. 22) → eschatological proclamation (vv. 27-31: 'all the families of the nations shall worship before you ... posterity shall serve him; it shall be told of the Lord to the coming generation'). The Psalm functions in NT usage not as a prediction of specific details but as the divinely-given interpretive framework for understanding the cross: suffering-unto-vindication, abandonment-unto-universal-proclamation.
2: 'O my God, I cry out by day, but you do not answer; by night I find no relief' — in the ancient world, divine silence was the gravest form of rejection. The day/night merism (continuous crying at both periods) indicates that the petitioner has exhausted every appropriate time of prayer without response. ANE petition texts regularly describe the persistent supplication of the worshiper and the expectation of divine reply; the failure to receive an answer implied either divine anger or divine absence. The Psalm names this experience honestly rather than theologizing it away.
3: 'But you are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel' — the theological pivot between vv. 1-2 (unanswered prayer) and vv. 4-5 (the ancestors' answered prayer). YHWH's holiness and transcendence do not negate the relationship but explain why his ways exceed understanding. 'Enthroned on the praises of Israel' (yoshev tehillot yisrael) — YHWH is not indifferent to worship; he dwells within it. The covenant history of Israel's praise is itself the throne from which YHWH exercises his faithfulness.
4: 'In you our ancestors put their trust; they trusted, and you rescued them' — the appeal to covenant history as the warrant for present petition. The patriarchs, the Exodus generation, and the judges-era community all experienced divine rescue following trust. In Israelite prayer, historical precedent was legally relevant: YHWH's past faithfulness obligated future faithfulness under the same covenant terms. The ancestors' experience of deliverance is a data-point in the psalmist's case.
5: 'They cried to you and were delivered; they trusted in you and were not put to shame' — the shame/honor dimension: in the ancient Near Eastern honor-shame culture, having one's trust vindicated was public honor; having it betrayed was public shame. The ancestors who trusted YHWH were not shamed — their trust was publicly rewarded. This is the precedent the psalmist invokes: YHWH has a documented record of not shaming those who trust him.
6: 'But I am a worm and not a man, scorned by everyone and despised by the people' — extreme social dehumanization. In the honor-shame world, social contempt was a form of death: a person stripped of honor and reduced to the status of a contemptible creature (tola'at, a worm — among the lowliest of creatures) had no standing in the community. The contrast with vv. 4-5 is deliberate: the ancestors were honored by being rescued; this petitioner is being treated as sub-human. The reversal from vv. 9-10 (YHWH's favored one from birth) to v. 6 (socially abased) is the Psalm's central tension.
7: 'All who see me mock me; they sneer and shake their heads' — the head-shaking gesture (yani'u bero'sh) was the conventional gesture of derision in ancient Israel and the ANE (cf. Lam 2:15: passersby 'clap their hands at you; they hiss and shake their heads at the daughter of Jerusalem'; Jer 18:16; Job 16:4). The mockery gesture was not merely emotional contempt but a social performance that communicated to all observers that the person was beneath consideration.
8: 'He trusted in the LORD; let the LORD rescue him' — the logic of the mockers mirrors the exact reasoning of the passersby at the crucifixion (Matt 27:39-43: 'He trusts in God; let God rescue him now, if he wants him'). The taunt assumes that divine favor = divine rescue; the absence of rescue = absence of divine favor. This was the standard ANE interpretation of unremedied suffering: if the deity had not intervened, the petitioner had been abandoned. The taunt is theologically coherent within its framework and therefore devastating.
9: 'Yet you drew me out of the womb; you made me trust in you at my mother's breasts' — the birth-covenant: the psalmist's relationship with YHWH was not self-initiated but divinely given at the moment of birth. The language parallels the Servant of YHWH in Isaiah (Isa 49:1: 'The LORD called me before I was born; from my mother's womb he named my name'). Divine care from birth established a relationship that the present abandonment must be interpreted within, not against.
10: 'Upon you I was cast from birth; from my mother's womb you have been my God' — the casting (hashlik) onto YHWH at birth implies that the dependence on YHWH was not a later choice but the original condition of the psalmist's existence. In ancient Israel, the birth and naming of a child was a communally significant event; the child was placed in the care of YHWH from that moment. The psalmist presses this foundational covenant as the argument for divine response: you have been my God since birth — how can you be absent now?
11: 'Do not be far from me, for trouble is near and there is no one to help' — the petition repeating the spatial contrast: YHWH is described as 'far' (vv. 1, 11, 19) while trouble is 'near.' In the patron-client system of ancient Israel, the patron's physical proximity was necessary for protection; a patron who was absent (whether geographically or relationally) could not defend the client. The declaration 'there is no one to help' is the client's formal statement that all human patrons have abandoned him, leaving YHWH as the only recourse.
12: 'Many bulls surround me; powerful bulls of Bashan encircle me' — Bashan was the fertile plateau east of the Jordan, famous for its rich pastureland (Deut 32:14; Amos 4:1; Ezek 39:18). The well-fed cattle of Bashan were among the largest and most formidable animals in the ancient Near East. 'Bulls of Bashan' as an image of powerful, well-fed enemies encircling the powerless psalmist contrasts the psalmist's wasted condition (vv. 14-15) with his opponents' abundance. The bull was also a royal and divine symbol in Canaanite religion (Baal as bull); the Psalm's enemies are as imposing as the symbols of opposing power.
13: 'They open wide their jaws against me like a roaring, ravening lion' — the lion (aryeh) was the supreme predator in ancient Israel; its roar was both a literal sound and an image of irresistible power. The combination of bulls (powerful, encircling) and lion (rending, devouring) creates a compound image of the threat from all directions — surrounded and about to be consumed. Both animals were associated with royal power (Davidic lion imagery of 1 Kgs 10:19-20; the lion of Judah, Gen 49:9), here turned against the Davidic king.
14: 'I am poured out like water, and all my bones are disjointed; my heart has turned to wax, melting within me' — extreme physical prostration. 'Poured out like water' = utter dissolution, inability to gather oneself. 'Bones disjointed' = the structural collapse of the body. 'Heart turned to wax' — the heart (lev) was understood as the seat of will and strength; its melting describes the complete collapse of courage and determination. These are physiological markers of extreme distress, possibly including shock or severe illness, described in the concrete bodily language of ancient medicine.
15: 'My strength is dried up like a fragment of pottery; my tongue sticks to the roof of my mouth; you lay me in the dust of death' — extreme dehydration and proximity to death. A pottery shard without moisture is absolutely brittle; the tongue adhering to the palate is a classic symptom of severe dehydration in the ancient climate. 'The dust of death' (aphar mavet) is the grave: in the ancient cosmology, the dead returned to the dust from which they came (Gen 3:19). The psalmist is at the threshold of Sheol.
16: 'Dogs surround me; a mob of evildoers encircles me; they have pierced my hands and feet' — dogs in the ancient Near East were scavengers, not pets; they were associated with shame and uncleanness (1 Kgs 21:19: dogs licking Naboth's blood as divine retribution; Rev 22:15: dogs outside the holy city). A 'mob of dogs' surrounding the dying person is the image of the shameful, witnessed, public death. The 'piercing' of hands and feet: the Hebrew text is difficult (MT reads 'like a lion'); the LXX reads 'they have pierced' (ōruxan), which the NT Passion narratives presuppose.
17: 'I can count all my bones; they stare and gloat over me' — visible emaciation as public shame. In the ancient world, physical deterioration was publicly visible (people lived in close proximity; the body was not hidden away). Counting visible bones = extreme wasting. The enemies 'stare and gloat' (yab'itu yir'u-bi) — witnessing the suffering as entertainment or as confirmation of deserved judgment. Public exposure of the suffering body was itself a component of punishment and shame in both Israelite and ANE legal practice.
18: 'They divide my garments among themselves and cast lots for my clothing' — the dividing of a condemned person's clothing was the conqueror's right and the loser's ultimate dispossession. In Israelite law, the condemned man's property reverted to the community or the executor. The casting of lots for specific articles was a standard practice when multiple parties wanted the same object (cf. Num 26:55-56: lots for the division of the land; Neh 10:34: lots for wood-offering). John 19:23-24 applies this verse explicitly to the soldiers' division of Jesus's garments at the crucifixion.
19: 'But you, LORD, do not stay far away! You are my strength; come quickly to help me!' — the petition following the extended lament. The 'but you' (ve-attah) is the pivot from description of suffering to direct address to YHWH. 'Far away' continues the spatial motif: YHWH who seemed distant is now directly addressed. The urgency ('come quickly') mirrors the urgency of the military situation described in vv. 12-18. The psalmist does not argue that YHWH should act because the suffering is unjust but because YHWH is 'my strength' — the covenant patron who has committed to support the client.
20: 'Rescue my life from the sword, my precious soul from the clutch of the dog!' — the sword (cherev) was the primary weapon of execution in ancient Israel; rescue from the sword = rescue from judicial or military death. 'The clutch of the dog' — returned to the dog image of v. 16: dogs as scavengers who consume the dead. 'My precious soul (yechidati)' — literally 'my only one,' the unique and irreplaceable self; the same word used for Isaac in Gen 22:2 ('your only son, the one you love'). The plea is for the preservation of the one irreplaceable life YHWH gave.
21: 'Rescue me from the lion's jaws! You have answered me from the horns of the wild oxen' — the 'wild ox' (re'em) was a fearsome beast of ancient Israel (mentioned in Num 23:22; Deut 33:17; Job 39:9-10). The horns of the wild ox were a standard image of irresistible power. 'You have answered me' — a sudden perfect tense breaks through the lament: the divine answer has already come. This pivot from petition to past-tense confidence in v. 21b is the hinge of the entire Psalm, after which the second half (praise and proclamation) flows.
22: 'I will proclaim your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you' — the transition from individual lament to communal proclamation. The 'brothers' (achim) are the covenant community gathered for worship. In Second Temple Judaism, public proclamation of YHWH's saving acts was performed in the assembly (qahal) as part of the thanksgiving sacrifice (todah) ceremony: the rescued person told the congregation of his deliverance. Hebrews 2:12 applies this verse directly to Christ proclaiming the Father's name to his brothers after resurrection.
23: 'You who fear the LORD, praise him! All descendants of Jacob, honor him! Stand in awe of him, all descendants of Israel!' — the three-fold call to the covenant community to share in the rescued person's praise. The 'fear of the LORD,' honoring, and awe are three distinct postures of covenant worship ranging from reverent response to formal honor to solemn awe. The movement from 'those who fear the LORD' to 'descendants of Jacob' to 'descendants of Israel' is a widening circle: the devout, then the tribal community, then all of Israel — all summoned to corporate praise.
24: 'For he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but listened when he cried out to him' — the key theological affirmation: the appearance of divine abandonment (vv. 1-2, 11, 19) was exactly that — appearance, not reality. YHWH did not hide his face (the conventional metaphor for divine withdrawal of favor and presence). The 'face' (panim) of YHWH signifies covenant presence; its hiddenness means relational distance. That YHWH did not hide his face means the covenant was never broken even when it felt broken.
25: 'My praise in the great congregation comes from you; I will fulfill my vows before those who fear him' — the todah (thanksgiving) vow ceremony: when in distress, the ancient Israelite would vow to offer a thanksgiving sacrifice and public proclamation if YHWH delivered him (cf. Ps 116:12-19). The fulfillment of the vow was performed publicly 'before those who fear him' — the assembled worshipers. The rescued person's testimony was part of the corporate liturgy of thanksgiving.
26: 'The humble will eat and be satisfied; those who seek the LORD will praise him' — the communal dimension of the todah sacrifice: after offering the animal, the rescued person shared the meat with the poor (anavim, the humble). The feast following the thanksgiving sacrifice was a public redistribution of the gift of deliverance: the saved person feeding the hungry. The connection between individual rescue and communal generosity is built into the liturgical structure of thanksgiving worship.
27: 'All the ends of the earth will remember and turn to the LORD, and all the families of the nations will bow down before him' — the remarkable eschatological expansion of the Psalm's scope: from one person's distress (v. 1) to the entire world's worship. The 'ends of the earth' (afsei-arets) encompassing all nations bowing before YHWH is the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3: 'all families of the earth shall be blessed through you') in Psalmic form. A personal lament becomes a universal promise — one of the widest trajectories in all of Scripture.
28: 'For dominion belongs to the LORD, and he rules over the nations' — the theological ground of the universal worship envisioned in v. 27: YHWH's sovereignty is not limited to Israel or Zion but extends over all peoples. This is the same universalism as Ps 24:1 ('the earth belongs to the LORD and all its fullness'). The eschatological vision of all nations worshiping YHWH rests on the foundation of his actual cosmic sovereignty — it is not an aspiration but the expression of what already is cosmically true.
29: 'All the prosperous of the earth will feast and worship; before him all who go down to the dust will bow — even those who cannot preserve their own life' — the comprehensive scope: both the prosperous (deshnei eretz) and the dying ('those who go down to the dust') worship. This is the full spectrum of humanity: the powerful and the utterly powerless both bow before YHWH. 'Those who cannot preserve their own life' may refer to the critically ill or the dying — even those unable to perform worship by conventional means are included in the eschatological assembly.
30: 'Descendants will serve him; the Lord will be declared to the coming generation' — the generational transmission of YHWH's saving acts was the core mechanism of covenant memory in ancient Israel: Deut 6:20-25 commands parents to tell their children the Exodus story when asked; Ps 78:2-4 describes the pattern of one generation teaching the next. The rescue narrated in Ps 22 is to be declared (yesuppar) to the next generation — becoming part of the ongoing covenant testimony.
31: 'They will come and proclaim his saving acts to a people not yet born, declaring that he has done it' — the reach forward in time: the testimony of YHWH's saving act in this Psalm is projected to future generations not yet in existence. 'He has done it' (ki asah) is the conclusion of the proclamation — a completed-act declaration of divine faithfulness. This phrase has been identified as the functional equivalent of Jesus's 'It is finished' (tetelestai, John 19:30), which the early church saw as the declaration that the Ps 22 pattern was complete.
